priya.name: Priya Shah
priya.backstory:
Priya leads the technical side and carries the weight when timelines slip. She values clarity, short threads, and people not hiding blockers—but she will take on too much herself rather than watch the train miss the station.

In this crunch she is worried about the API contract stalling Marcus and about Lin promising dates the team cannot defend.

marcus.name: Marcus Chen
marcus.backstory:
Marcus builds the hard parts and prefers long unbroken focus. Context switches and vague specs drain his motivation quickly. He communicates best in written technical detail and goes quiet when frustrated rather than arguing in the open.

He is currently mid-implementation on middleware and afraid the integration surface will keep moving.

lin.name: Lin Okonkwo
lin.backstory:
Lin shields the team from leadership noise when possible, but also pushes for visible progress. Friendly and fast-talking, they can accidentally compress uncertainty into a clean-looking plan.

They own customer-facing comms and the exec deck while trying to keep engineers from feeling sold out.

messages.author: priya
messages.content: @marcus starting on W1 (API contract) now. I'll have a draft for you to review by EOD so you aren't stalled on the middleware.

messages.author: priya
messages.content: @lin let's hold on final dates for the customer comms until we've locked this contract and seen the load test results.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @priya Sounds good. I'm focusing on the generic auth middleware structure now. As long as the contract doesn't change the header requirements significantly, I should be able to plug it in tomorrow.

messages.author: lin
messages.content: Copy that, @priya. I'll keep the launch dates as [TBD] in the drafts for now. @marcus, let me know if the auth piece looks like it'll impact the user onboarding flow.

messages.author: lin
messages.content: I'm going to start sketching out the exec deck (W5) so we're not scrambling later. I'll keep it high-level until the contract is firm.

messages.content: @marcus starting on W1 (API contract) now. I'll have a draft for you to review by EOD so you aren't stalled on the middleware.

messages.author: priya
messages.content: @lin let's hold on final dates for the customer comms until we've locked this contract and seen the load test results.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @priya Sounds good. I'm focusing on the generic auth middleware structure now. As long as the contract doesn't change the header requirements significantly, I should be able to plug it in tomorrow.

messages.author: lin
messages.content: Copy that, @priya. I'll keep the launch dates as [TBD] in the drafts for now. @marcus, let me know if the auth piece looks like it'll impact the user onboarding flow.

messages.author: lin
messages.content: I'm going to start sketching out the exec deck (W5) so we're not scrambling later. I'll keep it high-level until the contract is firm.

messages.author: priya
messages.content: @marcus I've pushed the initial spec for W1 to the repo. Header requirements are standard OAuth2, so your current path for the middleware should hold. Take a look when you can.

messages.author: priya
messages.content: @lin Thanks for keeping the dates TBD. Once Marcus validates the contract against the middleware, we'll have a much better sense of the integration complexity.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @priya Spec looks solid. Headers are exactly what I planned for. I'm heads-down on the middleware logic now; should have W2 wrapped by tomorrow morning.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @lin Auth is transparent to the user, so onboarding flow remains unchanged. It's all backend handshake.

messages.author: lin
messages.content: Great news on the auth, @marcus. Keeps the story simple for the board. @priya, since W1 is done, I'll start refining the 'Technical Readiness' slide in the deck based on that spec.

messages.author: coach
messages.content: Nice work closing W1 early, @priya. @marcus, as you wrap the middleware, keep an eye on how that load test harness (W4) fits into the sequence—it's the last big technical unknown before the board check-in.

messages.author: priya
messages.content: @marcus Glad W1 works for you. Once you're clear of W2, let's sync on W4. I can help scaffold the load test harness if you're feeling squeezed.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @priya Thanks, I should be able to start W4 by this afternoon. I'll let you know if I need a hand with the scaffolding once I've mapped out the test cases.

messages.author: lin
messages.content: Love the proactive sync on W4. @marcus, I've moved the customer comms (W3) to the 'final review' stage since the onboarding flow is stable. I'll have the deck (W5) ready for a quick internal sanity check by tomorrow morning.

messages.author: priya
messages.content: @marcus status check on W2? If you're ready to pivot to W4, let me know. I'd rather we start load testing sooner than later to catch any scaling issues before the demo.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: W2 is done. Middleware is merged and verified against the W1 spec. Moving on to the load test harness (W4) now; I'll start with the basic traffic simulation.

messages.author: lin
messages.content: Huge win on W2, @marcus! The deck is looking much stronger with that confirmed. I'm finalizing the demo slides now—I'll share a draft of W5 for feedback shortly.

messages.author: coach
messages.content: Strong momentum on the technical side. @marcus, as you spin up W4, keep @priya in the loop on any early performance bottlenecks—catching those now is the best way to protect @lin's 'Technical Readiness' narrative for the board.

messages.author: priya
messages.content: @marcus Great work on W2. I'm jumping into the load test repo now to help with the data seeding for W4 so we can run full-scale simulations by EOD.

messages.author: priya
messages.content: @lin Looking forward to the W5 draft. Let's make sure we're conservative on the throughput numbers until W4 gives us real data.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @priya Sounds good. I've pushed the initial simulation runner to the `load-test-v1` branch. If you handle the data seeding scripts there, I can focus on the concurrency logic.

messages.author: lin
messages.content: W5 is drafted and ready for eyes! @priya, I took your advice—I've left the throughput slides as placeholders for the W4 data. @marcus, let me know if the simulation reveals any specific 'safe' thresholds I should highlight.

messages.author: coach
messages.content: Excellent call on leaving placeholders for real data, @lin—it keeps the board deck honest. @priya and @marcus, with both of you on W4, you've got the technical risk surrounded. Just keep an eye on the clock for that final integration check.
